subject: UX & Research
title: The Multiple Comparisons Problem
description: A short lesson to help you apply The Multiple Comparisons Problem.

hook.text: You test 20 metrics in one experiment. One shows significance. Is it real? Probably not — it's likely noise.

buildup.text: At p=0.05, you expect 1 in 20 metrics to be significant by pure chance. Test 20 metrics and you'll find one.

discovery.text: This is why you must choose your primary metric before the test starts. Secondary metrics are exploratory, not decisive.

twist.text: Corrections like Bonferroni adjust the threshold: test 20 metrics and require p < 0.0025 instead of 0.05.

climax.text: Pre-register your hypothesis and primary metric. Exploring data after the test is useful, but not conclusive.

punchline.text: Choose your key metric before testing. Post-hoc finds are just hypotheses.

quiz.question: Why should you choose your primary metric before running an A/B test?
options: Testing many metrics inflates the chance of finding a false positive by pure luck
Testing platforms only allow one metric at a time
